title: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil

items.subtopic: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophilia | INTRODUCTION AND DEFINITION | OFFENDING ANTIGENS
items.title: Identifying Offending Antigens in Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis (HP)
items.content: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis is caused by inhalational exposure to various antigens leading to an inflammatory response. Sensitization, indicated by specific circulating IgG antibodies, is necessary but not sufficient for HP development.
keyPoints: Antigens implicated in HP include fungal, bacterial, mycobacterial, bird-derived, and chemical sources.
Categories at risk: farmers (farmer's lung), bird owners (bird fancier's lung), industrial workers (chemical worker's lung), hot tub users (hot tub lung).
Specific antigens: thermophilic actinomycetes, Aspergillus species, feathers, droppings, serum proteins, diphenylmethane diisocyanate, toluene diisocyanate.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophilia | INTRODUCTION AND DEFINITION | OFFENDING ANTIGENS

items.subtopic: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophilia | INTRODUCTION AND DEFINITION | OFFENDING ANTIGENS
items.title: Risk Factors and Geographic Variability in Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis (HP)
items.content: The incidence of HP varies by geography, occupation, avocation, and environment. A decreased risk is observed in smokers, though the reason for this remains unexplained.
keyPoints: HP incidence is variable based on geographic location, occupational exposure, and environmental factors.
Decreased risk of developing HP is noted among smokers, but the mechanism behind this phenomenon is not understood.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophilia | INTRODUCTION AND DEFINITION | OFFENDING ANTIGENS

items.title: HP Pathophysiology: Immune-Mediated and Genetic Factors
items.content: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis (HP) is an immune-mediated condition triggered by inhaled antigens, involving dysregulated T_H1 and T_H17 responses. Both adaptive immunity (evidenced by IgG antibodies against specific antigens) and innate immunity (involving Toll-like receptors and MyD88 activation leading to neutrophil recruitment) play roles in its pathophysiology. Genetic factors include polymorphisms in antigen processing genes like TAP1, MHC type II, and a shared polymorphism with IPF in the mucin gene MUC5B.
keyPoints: HP involves dysregulated T_H1 and T_H17 immune responses.
Adaptive immunity is indicated by precipitating IgG antibodies against specific antigens.
Innate immunity contributes through Toll-like receptors and MyD88 activation, leading to neutrophil recruitment.
Polymorphisms in genes like TAP1, MHC type II, and mucin gene MUC5B are associated with HP.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: HP Clinical Presentation: Variability and Forms
items.content: The clinical presentation of HP is highly variable due to differences in antigens, exposure intensity, and duration. Traditionally categorized as acute, subacute, or chronic, recent guidelines suggest classifying it as non-fibrotic or fibrotic. Acute symptoms can be intense but resolve quickly without further antigen exposure, while chronic HP presents more insidiously with progressive dyspnea, cough, fatigue, weight loss, and clubbing of digits.
keyPoints: HP presentation varies based on antigens, exposure intensity, and duration.
Acute symptoms may resolve within hours to days if no further antigen exposure occurs.
Chronic HP presents with progressive dyspnea, cough, fatigue, weight loss, and clubbing of digits.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: HP Outcomes: Impact of Chronicity and Antigen Avoidance
items.content: The outcome of HP depends on its progression. Non-chronic HP has a favorable prognosis with likely resolution upon antigen avoidance, while chronic HP leading to lung fibrosis has a poorer prognosis similar to IPF. Fibrotic lung disease can result from chronic exposure to bird antigens, and an emphysematous phenotype may occur in farmer's lung.
keyPoints: Non-chronic HP resolves with antigen avoidance, having a favorable outcome.
Chronic HP leading to fibrosis has a poor prognosis similar to IPF.
Bird antigen exposure can lead to fibrotic lung disease; farmer's lung may present with an emphysematous phenotype.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Role of Chest Imaging in Diagnosing HP
items.content: Chest imaging is crucial for diagnosing Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis (HP), though findings can be nonspecific. In nonfibrotic HP, transient opacities may resolve upon antigen removal, while fibrotic HP shows more persistent abnormalities.
keyPoints: Chest x-ray findings in HP are often nonspecific and may lack discernible abnormalities.
Nonfibrotic HP can show ill-defined micronodular opacities or hazy ground-glass airspace opacities that resolve with antigen removal.
Fibrotic HP shows fibrotic changes on chest radiograph, which may be difficult to distinguish from IPF.
HRCT in nonfibrotic HP often reveals ground-glass opacities and centrilobular nodules; expiratory images may show air trapping due to small airway involvement.
Reticular changes and traction bronchiectasis are seen in fibrotic HP, with subpleural honeycombing similar to IPF but sparing the lung bases.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Importance of Pulmonary Function Testing in HP
items.content: Pulmonary function tests (PFTs) help characterize physiologic impairment and monitor response to treatment in HP, although they do not establish the diagnosis.
keyPoints: HP can present with either restrictive or obstructive PFT patterns.
The pattern of PFT change is not diagnostic for HP but useful for assessing physiologic impairment.
Diffusion capacity for carbon monoxide may be significantly impaired, especially in fibrotic HP.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Use of Serology and Bronchoscopy in Diagnosing HP
items.content: Serum precipitins and bronchoscopy with BAL are adjuncts in diagnosing HP, though neither is definitive on its own.
keyPoints: IgG antibodies against specific antigens can aid diagnosis but are not sufficient alone due to false negatives and presence in asymptomatic individuals.
BAL lymphocytosis is characteristic of HP; however, a lower threshold for lymphocyte percentage should be used in active smokers.
Most cases of HP have a CD4+/CD8+ lymphocyte ratio of <1, but this finding has limited diagnostic utility.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Prediction Rule for Diagnosing HP
items.content: A clinical prediction rule developed by the HP Study Group identifies six predictors for diagnosing hypersensitivity pneumonitis, with exposure to a known antigen being the strongest predictor.
keyPoints: Strongest predictor: Exposure to an antigen known to cause HP.
Other predictors include serum precipitins, recurrent symptoms, symptom onset 4–8 hours after exposure, crackles on inspiration, and weight loss.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis

items.title: Differential Diagnosis of HP
items.content: Differentiating hypersensitivity pneumonitis from other conditions like respiratory infections, interstitial lung diseases, and sarcoidosis requires careful history taking and consideration of specific clinical features.
keyPoints: HP must be differentiated from IPF, NSIP, and sarcoidosis.
Sarcoidosis may present with hilar adenopathy and involvement of organs other than the lungs.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis

items.title: Treatment Approach for HP
items.content: The primary treatment for hypersensitivity pneumonitis is antigen avoidance, with glucocorticoid therapy used in more severe cases to accelerate symptom resolution.
keyPoints: Mainstay of treatment: Antigen avoidance.
Glucocorticoid therapy may be initiated at 0.5–1 mg/kg of ideal body weight per day (not exceeding 60 mg/d) for 1-2 weeks, followed by a taper over 2-6 weeks.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PULMONARY INFILTRATES WITH EOSINOPHILIA | CLASSIFYING PULMONARY INFILTRATES WITH EOSINOPHILIA AND GENERAL APPROACH
items.title: Understanding Geographic Variability in Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis (HP)
items.content: The risk populations for HP vary globally due to specific local occupational, avocational, and environmental factors. Recognizing geographically limited forms of HP is crucial for accurate diagnosis.
keyPoints: Populations at risk for HP vary based on local factors.
Examples include summer-type pneumonitis in Japan and suberosis in cork workers in Portugal and Spain.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PULMONARY INFILTRATES WITH EOSINOPHILIA | CLASSIFYING PULMONARY INFILTRATES WITH EOSINOPHILIA AND GENERAL APPROACH

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PULMONARY INFILTRATES WITH EOSINOPHILIA | CLASSIFYING PULMONARY INFILTRATES WITH EOSINOPHILIA AND GENERAL APPROACH
items.title: Approach to Classifying Pulmonary Eosinophilic Syndromes
items.content: Distinguishing between primary and secondary pulmonary eosinophilic disorders is essential. A detailed history, including pharmacologic, occupational, and environmental exposures, aids in identifying the underlying cause.
keyPoints: Differentiate between primary pulmonary eosinophilic lung disorders and those secondary to causes like drug reactions or infections.
Detailed patient history is crucial for diagnosis.
Laboratory testing, spirometry, radiographic imaging, and biopsies may be necessary.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PULMONARY INFILTRATES WITH EOSINOPHILIA | CLASSIFYING PULMONARY INFILTRATES WITH EOSINOPHILIA AND GENERAL APPROACH

items.title: Diagnosis and Etiology of Acute Eosinophilic Pneumonia
items.content: Acute eosinophilic pneumonia is characterized by acute respiratory failure, diffuse pulmonary infiltrates, and significant pulmonary eosinophilia. It often presents with symptoms similar to ARDS but can be distinguished by bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) showing >25% eosinophils. The etiology remains unclear, though recent smoking or vaping has been linked in some cases.
keyPoints: BAL reveals >25% eosinophils for diagnosis
Symptoms include cough, dyspnea, malaise, myalgias, night sweats, pleuritic chest pain
Commonly affects males aged 20-40 without asthma history
Linked to recent smoking or vaping in some cases
Peripheral eosinophilia often occurs with mean counts of 1700 between 7 and 30 days post-onset
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | ACUTE EOSINOPHILIC PNEUMONIA

items.title: Clinical Course and Treatment Response in Acute Eosinophilic Pneumonia
items.content: Acute eosinophilic pneumonia typically requires intensive care with respiratory support. It is distinguished by a lack of multisystem organ failure beyond respiratory issues, high corticosteroid responsiveness, and excellent prognosis. Patients generally achieve full recovery without recurrence or sequelae within weeks after starting treatment.
keyPoints: Most patients need ICU admission and mechanical ventilation
Lack of multisystem organ failure apart from respiratory failure
Highly responsive to corticosteroids with excellent prognosis
Complete clinical and radiographic recovery occurs in almost all patients
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | ACUTE EOSINOPHILIC PNEUMONIA

items.title: Characteristics and Presentation of Chronic Eosinophilic Pneumonia
items.content: Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia is a more indolent syndrome compared to its acute counterpart, characterized by pulmonary infiltrates and significant eosinophilia in both tissue and blood. It predominantly affects female nonsmokers with a mean age of 45 years. Patients typically present with subacute symptoms over weeks to months including cough, low-grade fevers, progressive dyspnea, weight loss, wheezing, malaise, night sweats, and characteristic radiographic findings.
keyPoints: Most patients are female nonsmokers with a mean age of 45 years.
Patients present with subacute symptoms over weeks to months: cough, low-grade fevers, progressive dyspnea, weight loss, wheezing, malaise, night sweats.
<25% of patients have the 'photographic negative pulmonary edema' appearance on chest x-ray and CT.
Other radiographic findings include atelectasis, pleural effusions, lymphadenopathy, and septal line thickening.
Almost 90% of patients have peripheral eosinophilia with mean counts over 30% of total white blood cell count.
BAL eosinophilia is a distinguishing feature with mean BAL eosinophil counts around 60%.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · CHRONIC EOSINOPHILIC PNEUMONIA

items.title: Diagnostic and Management Considerations in Chronic Eosinophilic Pneumonia
items.content: Diagnosis of chronic eosinophilic pneumonia often relies on clinical presentation, peripheral and BAL eosinophilia, and radiographic findings. While lung biopsy is not typically required, it may show accumulation of eosinophils and histiocytes with minimal fibrosis. Treatment involves corticosteroids which lead to rapid resolution of symptoms and eosinophilia. However, more than 50% of patients experience relapse and may require prolonged corticosteroid therapy.
keyPoints: Increased ESR, C-reactive protein, platelets, and IgE are other laboratory features.
Lung biopsy shows accumulation of eosinophils and histiocytes with minimal fibrosis; BAL eosinophilia is a distinguishing feature.
Rapid response to corticosteroids with quick resolution of peripheral and BAL eosinophilia and improvement in symptoms.
>50% of patients relapse, requiring prolonged courses of corticosteroids for months to years.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · CHRONIC EOSINOPHILIC PNEUMONIA

items.title: Diagnosis and Phases of EGPA
items.content: EGPA is characterized by a phasic progression from asthma to eosinophilic infiltrates, culminating in vasculitis. The prodromal phase involves asthma and allergic rhinitis, the eosinophilic infiltrative phase includes peripheral eosinophilia and organ infiltration, and the vasculitic phase presents with systemic symptoms.
keyPoints: EGPA is characterized by asthma, peripheral eosinophilia, neuropathy, pulmonary infiltrates, paranasal sinus abnormality, and eosinophilic vasculitis.
Mean age at diagnosis is 48 years; time between asthma diagnosis and vasculitis is 9 years on average.
Phasic progression: prodromal (asthma/allergic rhinitis), eosinophilic infiltrative (peripheral eosinophilia/organ infiltration), vasculitic (constitutional symptoms).
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · EOSINOPHILIC GRANULOMATOSIS WITH POLYANGIITIS (EGPA) (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Manifestations of EGPA
items.content: EGPA affects multiple organ systems, with common symptoms including severe asthma, weight loss, myalgias, and polyarthralgias. Neurologic involvement is frequent, often presenting as mononeuritis multiplex, while cardiac complications can be severe but partially reversible.
keyPoints: Respiratory: Severe asthma, cough, dyspnea, sinusitis, allergic rhinitis; alveolar hemorrhage and hemoptysis possible.
Neurologic: Over 75% have manifestations like mononeuritis multiplex (commonly peroneal nerve), cerebral hemorrhage/infarction.
Cardiovascular: Granulomas, vasculitis, myocardial damage; cardiomyopathy and heart failure in up to half of patients.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · EOSINOPHILIC GRANULOMATOSIS WITH POLYANGIITIS (EGPA) (part 1)

items.title: Laboratory Findings in EGPA
items.content: Systemic eosinophilia is a hallmark of EGPA, with levels >1000 cells/μL or >10% of WBC count being diagnostic. ANCA positivity occurs in about one-third of patients, and nonspecific lab abnormalities include elevated ESR, anemia, and hypergammaglobulinemia.
keyPoints: Systemic eosinophilia (>1000 cells/μL or >10% of WBC) is a defining feature; present at diagnosis in >80% of patients.
ANCA positivity (~33%) with perinuclear staining pattern, often against myeloperoxidase.
Nonspecific abnormalities: elevated ESR, normochromic normocytic anemia, elevated IgE, hypergammaglobulinemia.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · EOSINOPHILIC GRANULOMATOSIS WITH POLYANGIITIS (EGPA) (part 1)

items.title: Radiographic Features of EGPA
items.content: Chest x-ray and CT findings are crucial in diagnosing EGPA, showing specific patterns that help differentiate it from other conditions.
keyPoints: Common chest x-ray abnormalities include bilateral, nonsegmental, patchy infiltrates that may migrate and appear interstitial or alveolar.
CT findings often show bilateral ground-glass opacity and airspace consolidation predominantly subpleural.
Additional CT features can include bronchial wall thickening, hyperinflation, interlobular septal thickening, lymph node enlargement, and pleural effusions.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · EOSINOPHILIC GRANULOMATOSIS WITH POLYANGIITIS (EGPA) (part 2)

items.title: Treatment and Prognosis of EGPA
items.content: Corticosteroids are the cornerstone of EGPA treatment, significantly improving survival rates. Additional therapies may be required for severe cases.
keyPoints: Untreated EGPA has a high mortality rate: up to 50% die within 3 months.
Treated patients have a >70% 6-year survival rate.
Common causes of death include heart failure, cerebral hemorrhage, renal failure, and GI bleeding.
Clinical remission is achieved in >90% of treated patients; ~25% may relapse due to corticosteroid tapering.
Cyclophosphamide reduces relapse incidence despite no difference in survival compared to untreated.
Anti-IL-5 therapies (mepolizumab, benralizumab) are effective corticosteroid-sparing agents.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · EOSINOPHILIC GRANULOMATOSIS WITH POLYANGIITIS (EGPA) (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Subtypes of Hypereosinophilic Syndromes (HES)
items.content: Hypereosinophilic syndromes are characterized by persistent eosinophilia greater than 1500 eosinophils/μL, associated with end organ damage or dysfunction, excluding secondary causes. The primary subtypes include myeloproliferative and lymphocytic variants. Myeloproliferative HES may involve genetic abnormalities such as Fip1L1-PDGFRα fusion due to a chromosomal deletion on 4q12, responsive to imatinib. Other mutations can affect PDGFRβ, JAK2, and FGFR1.
keyPoints: Persistent eosinophilia >1500 eosinophils/μL
End organ damage or dysfunction
Absence of secondary causes
Myeloproliferative variants: Fip1L1-PDGFRα fusion, responsive to imatinib
Other mutations: PDGFRβ, JAK2, FGFR1
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · HYPEREOSINOPHILIC SYNDROMES

items.title: Management and Response to Therapy in HES
items.content: Less than half of patients with pulmonary manifestations of HES respond to corticosteroids as first-line therapy. Myeloproliferative variants, especially those with Fip1L1-PDGFRA gene fusion, benefit from imatinib. Other treatment options include hydroxyurea, cyclosporine, and interferon. Anti-IL-5 therapies like mepolizumab have shown efficacy, while benralizumab is under investigation.
keyPoints: <50% response to corticosteroids
Imatinib effective for myeloproliferative variant with Fip1L1-PDGFRA fusion
Other treatments: hydroxyurea, cyclosporine, interferon
Anti-IL-5 therapy (mepolizumab) efficacy demonstrated
Benralizumab under investigation
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · HYPEREOSINOPHILIC SYNDROMES

items.title: Diagnosis of Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis (ABPA)
items.content: ABPA is an eosinophilic pulmonary disorder associated with asthma, characterized by elevated IgE levels and sensitivity to Aspergillus antigens. It should be considered in asthmatic patients unresponsive to standard treatments.
keyPoints: Predominant clinical presentation includes asthmatic phenotype and cough with brownish mucus plugs.
ABPA is a complication of cystic fibrosis.
Diagnosis requires elevated IgE levels (>500 IU/mL, often >1000 IU/mL) and sensitivity to Aspergillus antigens via skin test or specific IgE measurement.
Positive serum precipitins for Aspergillus or circulating specific IgG can be adjunct criteria.
Central bronchiectasis is a classic imaging finding but not necessary for diagnosis.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · ALLERGIC BRONCHOPULMONARY ASPERGILLOSIS

items.title: Management of Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis (ABPA)
items.content: Systemic glucocorticoids are used for ABPA when symptoms persist despite inhaled asthma therapies, with careful tapering over 3–6 months to mitigate steroid risks. Antifungal agents like itraconazole and voriconazole may also be considered.
keyPoints: Use systemic glucocorticoids for persistent symptomatic ABPA unresponsive to inhaled therapies.
Taper glucocorticoid courses over 3–6 months, balancing against prolonged steroid therapy risks.
Consider antifungal agents such as itraconazole and voriconazole.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · ALLERGIC BRONCHOPULMONARY ASPERGILLOSIS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a source of antigens implicated in causing hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP)?
options: Fungal sources such as Aspergillus species
Viral infections from common cold viruses
items.explanation: HP is caused by fungal, bacterial, mycobacterial, bird-derived, and chemical antigens; viral infections are not mentioned as a source.

items.question: Which occupational group is at risk for developing hypersensitivity pneumonitis due to exposure to bird-derived antigens?
items.explanation: Poultry worker's lung is caused by occupational exposure to bird-derived antigens.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophilia | INTRODUCTION AND DEFINITION | OFFENDING ANTIGENS

items.subtopic: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophilia | INTRODUCTION AND DEFINITION | OFFENDING ANTIGENS
items.question: What condition results from exposure to bacterial or fungal antigens in grain, moldy hay, or silage?
items.explanation: Farmer's lung is caused by exposure to bacterial or fungal antigens in grain, moldy hay, or silage.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophilia | INTRODUCTION AND DEFINITION | OFFENDING ANTIGENS

items.question: Which immune responses are dysregulated in hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP)?
items.explanation: HP is characterized by dysregulated $T_H1$ and $T_H17$ immune responses.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: What role do Toll-like receptors play in the pathophysiology of HP?
options: They activate downstream signaling proteins like MyD88 leading to neutrophil recruitment
items.explanation: Toll-like receptors and downstream signaling proteins such as MyD88 are activated in HP, leading to neutrophil recruitment.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: Which gene polymorphism is associated with both chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP) and idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF)?
items.explanation: A polymorphism in the mucin gene MUC5B is associated with both chronic HP and IPF.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: What type of lung disease can result from chronic exposure to bird antigens in HP?
items.explanation: Fibrotic lung disease is a potential feature of chronic HP due to exposure to bird antigens.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic finding on HRCT for nonfibrotic hypersensitivity pneumonitis?
options: Ground-glass airspace opacities and centrilobular nodules
items.explanation: HRCT in nonfibrotic HP typically shows ground-glass airspace opacities and centrilobular nodules.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: What is the significance of a CD4+/CD8+ lymphocyte ratio <1 in bronchoalveolar lavage for diagnosing HP?
options: It confirms the diagnosis of hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
It indicates an obstructive pattern on pulmonary function tests.
It suggests a characteristic finding but is not specific to HP.
It rules out other causes of interstitial lung disease.
items.explanation: A CD4+/CD8+ lymphocyte ratio <1 in BAL is characteristic of HP but not specific.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which statement about serum precipitins is true for diagnosing hypersensitivity pneumonitis?
options: They are always positive in symptomatic individuals.
Specific IgG antibodies can be useful but not definitive.
They are only used when HRCT findings are inconclusive.
items.explanation: Serum precipitins can aid diagnosis, but their presence alone is insufficient for confirmation.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following findings on chest x-ray might resolve with removal from the offending antigen in nonfibrotic HP?
options: Ill-defined micronodular opacities or hazy ground-glass airspace opacities
items.explanation: In nonfibrotic HP, chest x-ray findings such as ill-defined micronodular opacities or hazy ground-glass opacities may resolve with antigen removal.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a statistically significant predictor for hypersensitivity pneumonitis according to the HP Study Group?
options: Exposure to an antigen known to cause HP
Symptoms occurring 4–8 hours after antigen exposure
High levels of specific IgG antibodies
items.explanation: Specific IgG antibodies are not mentioned as a predictor for hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis

items.question: What is the primary treatment strategy for hypersensitivity pneumonitis?
items.explanation: The mainstay of treatment for HP is antigen avoidance.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis

items.question: Which condition must be differentiated from hypersensitivity pneumonitis due to the presence of pulmonary infiltrates and noncasing granulomas on biopsy?
items.explanation: Sarcoidosis is considered in the differential diagnosis of HP due to similar biopsy findings.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis

items.question: What distinguishes sarcoidosis from hypersensitivity pneumonitis in terms of radiographic and clinical features?
options: Prominent hilar adenopathy on chest x-ray
Symptoms occurring 4–8 hours after antigen exposure
items.explanation: Sarcoidosis may present with prominent hilar adenopathy, unlike hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis

options: Distinguishing between primary and secondary disorders
items.explanation: The first step is distinguishing between primary pulmonary eosinophilic lung disorders and those with eosinophilia that are secondary to a specific cause.

items.question: Which interleukin is hypothesized to play an etiologic role in eosinophilopoiesis and is targeted by monoclonal antibodies for treating certain eosinophilic conditions?
items.explanation: Interleukin 5 (IL-5) is involved in eosinophilopoiesis and targeted by monoclonal antibodies for treating eosinophilic conditions.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | ACUTE EOSINOPHILIC PNEUMONIA

items.question: What percentage of eosinophils in BAL fluid is indicative of acute eosinophilic pneumonia?
items.explanation: Acute eosinophilic pneumonia is indicated by >25% eosinophils in BAL fluid.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | ACUTE EOSINOPHILIC PNEUMONIA

items.question: Which of the following is a distinguishing feature of acute eosinophilic pneumonia compared to other pulmonary eosinophilic syndromes?
options: Presence of peripheral eosinophilia at presentation
Lack of response to respiratory support
items.explanation: Acute eosinophilic pneumonia is distinguished by a high degree of corticosteroid responsiveness.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | ACUTE EOSINOPHILIC PNEUMONIA

items.question: Which radiographic finding is pathognomonic of chronic eosinophilic pneumonia?
options: "Photographic negative pulmonary edema" appearance on chest x-ray and CT
items.explanation: The 'photographic negative pulmonary edema' is pathognomonic of chronic eosinophilic pneumonia.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · CHRONIC EOSINOPHILIC PNEUMONIA

items.question: What percentage of patients with chronic eosinophilic pneumonia have peripheral eosinophilia?
items.explanation: Almost 90% of patients with chronic eosinophilic pneumonia have peripheral eosinophilia.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · CHRONIC EOSINOPHILIC PNEUMONIA

items.question: What is the mean BAL eosinophil count in patients with chronic eosinophilic pneumonia?
items.explanation: Mean BAL eosinophil counts are ~60% in chronic eosinophilic pneumonia.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · CHRONIC EOSINOPHILIC PNEUMONIA

items.question: Which of the following is a defining feature of EGPA?
options: Eosinophilia >1000 cells/μL or >10% of peripheral WBC count
Presence of ANCA in all patients
Absence of systemic corticosteroid therapy response
items.explanation: Eosinophilia >1000 cells/μL or >10% of peripheral WBC count is a defining feature of EGPA.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · EOSINOPHILIC GRANULOMATOSIS WITH POLYANGIITIS (EGPA) (part 1)

items.question: What phase in the progression of EGPA is characterized by peripheral eosinophilia and tissue infiltration?
items.explanation: The eosinophilic infiltrative phase is characterized by peripheral eosinophilia and tissue infiltration.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · EOSINOPHILIC GRANULOMATOSIS WITH POLYANGIITIS (EGPA) (part 1)

items.question: Which organ system is NOT typically involved in EGPA?
items.explanation: The liver is not typically involved in EGPA.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · EOSINOPHILIC GRANULOMATOSIS WITH POLYANGIITIS (EGPA) (part 1)

items.question: What is a common neurologic manifestation of EGPA?
items.explanation: Mononeuritis multiplex is a common neurologic manifestation, but cerebral hemorrhage and peripheral neuropathy can also occur.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · EOSINOPHILIC GRANULOMATOSIS WITH POLYANGIITIS (EGPA) (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a common radiographic feature seen in EGPA on chest x-ray?
items.explanation: Chest x-ray abnormalities in EGPA commonly include bilateral, nonsegmental, patchy infiltrates.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · EOSINOPHILIC GRANULOMATOSIS WITH POLYANGIITIS (EGPA) (part 2)

items.question: What is the most common CT finding in patients with EGPA?
options: Bilateral ground-glass opacity and airspace consolidation predominantly subpleural
items.explanation: The most common CT findings in EGPA include bilateral ground-glass opacity and airspace consolidation that is predominantly subpleural.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · EOSINOPHILIC GRANULOMATOSIS WITH POLYANGIITIS (EGPA) (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following treatments has not been shown to provide additional benefit in EGPA management?
items.explanation: Plasma exchange has not been shown to provide any additional benefit in the management of EGPA.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · EOSINOPHILIC GRANULOMATOSIS WITH POLYANGIITIS (EGPA) (part 2)

items.question: Which genetic abnormality is associated with the myeloproliferative variant of hypereosinophilic syndromes (HES) and is often responsive to imatinib?
options: Fip1L1-PDGFRα fusion gene due to a chromosomal deletion on 4q12
items.explanation: The Fip1L1-PDGFRα fusion gene, resulting from a chromosomal deletion on 4q12, is associated with the myeloproliferative variant of HES and responds to imatinib.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · HYPEREOSINOPHILIC SYNDROMES

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common clinical finding in myeloproliferative hypereosinophilic syndromes (HES)?
items.explanation: Elevated IgE levels are associated with lymphocytic HES variants, not typically with myeloproliferative HES.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · HYPEREOSINOPHILIC SYNDROMES

items.question: Which treatment option is NOT mentioned as being effective for hypereosinophilic syndromes (HES)?
items.explanation: Rituximab is not mentioned as a treatment option for HES in the passage.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · HYPEREOSINOPHILIC SYNDROMES

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic laboratory finding in allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA)?
options: Elevated circulating levels of IgE (>500 IU/mL, often >1000 IU/mL)
Absence of skin test reactivity to Aspergillus antigens
items.explanation: ABPA is characterized by elevated circulating levels of IgE and prominent peripheral eosinophilia.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · ALLERGIC BRONCHOPULMONARY ASPERGILLOSIS

items.question: Which diagnostic criterion is NOT necessary for establishing a diagnosis of ABPA?
options: Elevated circulating levels of IgE (>500 IU/mL)
Sensitivity to Aspergillus antigens by skin test or specific IgE measurement
items.explanation: Central bronchiectasis is a classic finding but not necessary for diagnosing ABPA.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · ALLERGIC BRONCHOPULMONARY ASPERGILLOSIS

items.question: What is the primary treatment approach for symptomatic ABPA that does not respond to inhaled asthma therapies?
options: Antifungal agents such as itraconazole or voriconazole alone
Systemic glucocorticoids with a tapering course over 3–6 months
Immediate cessation of all asthma medications
items.explanation: Systemic glucocorticoids are used for symptomatic ABPA not responsive to inhaled therapies.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · ALLERGIC BRONCHOPULMONARY ASPERGILLOSIS

items.subtopic: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophilia | INTRODUCTION AND DEFINITION | OFFENDING ANTIGENS
items.statement: Sensitization to an inhaled antigen is sufficient alone for the development of hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
items.explanation: While sensitization is necessary, it alone is not sufficient for developing HP.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophilia | INTRODUCTION AND DEFINITION | OFFENDING ANTIGENS

items.subtopic: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophilia | INTRODUCTION AND DEFINITION | OFFENDING ANTIGENS
items.statement: Smokers have a decreased risk of developing hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
items.explanation: The passage notes an unexplained decreased risk of HP in smokers.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophilia | INTRODUCTION AND DEFINITION | OFFENDING ANTIGENS

items.statement: Chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP) can progress to lung fibrosis with a similar prognosis as idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF).
items.explanation: The disease progression of chronic HP to lung fibrosis with honeycombing and hypoxemic respiratory failure can mirror that seen in IPF, with a similar prognosis.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.statement: Antigen avoidance generally results in immediate resolution of symptoms for all forms of hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP).
items.explanation: While antigen avoidance can lead to symptom resolution, it occurs over a slower time course, on the order of weeks to months, than that seen with acute HP.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.statement: Pulmonary function tests are useful in establishing the diagnosis of hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
items.explanation: PFTs help characterize physiologic impairment but do not establish HP diagnosis.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.statement: Histologic specimens are necessary to diagnose hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
items.explanation: While helpful, histologic specimens are not absolutely required for HP diagnosis.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.statement: In fibrotic hypersensitivity pneumonitis, antifibrotic agents such as nintedanib or pirfenidone may be considered.
items.explanation: Antifibrotic agents are mentioned for consideration in cases with progressive lung fibrosis.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis

items.statement: Glucocorticoid therapy changes the long-term outcome in patients with nonfibrotic hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids do not change the long-term outcome but can accelerate symptom resolution.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PULMONARY INFILTRATES WITH EOSINOPHILIA | CLASSIFYING PULMONARY INFILTRATES WITH EOSINOPHILIA AND GENERAL APPROACH
items.statement: Eosinophils are abnormal constituents of the lungs in pulmonary eosinophilic syndromes.
items.explanation: Eosinophils are normal constituents of the lungs, but their increased presence characterizes pulmonary eosinophilic syndromes.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PULMONARY INFILTRATES WITH EOSINOPHILIA | CLASSIFYING PULMONARY INFILTRATES WITH EOSINOPHILIA AND GENERAL APPROACH

items.subtopic: GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PULMONARY INFILTRATES WITH EOSINOPHILIA | CLASSIFYING PULMONARY INFILTRATES WITH EOSINOPHILIA AND GENERAL APPROACH
items.statement: A detailed history can help elucidate the underlying disease in pulmonary eosinophilic syndromes.
items.explanation: A detailed history is crucial for discerning the specific diagnosis among pulmonary eosinophilic syndromes.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PULMONARY INFILTRATES WITH EOSINOPHILIA | CLASSIFYING PULMONARY INFILTRATES WITH EOSINOPHILIA AND GENERAL APPROACH

items.statement: Acute eosinophilic pneumonia often presents with peripheral eosinophilia at the time of diagnosis.
items.explanation: Peripheral eosinophilia is not typically present at presentation but may occur 7 to 30 days after disease onset.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | ACUTE EOSINOPHILIC PNEUMONIA

items.statement: Acute eosinophilic pneumonia can be definitively diagnosed with a lung biopsy.
items.explanation: A lung biopsy is generally not necessary to establish the diagnosis of acute eosinophilic pneumonia.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | ACUTE EOSINOPHILIC PNEUMONIA

items.statement: Patients with chronic eosinophilic pneumonia typically develop acute respiratory failure and significant hypoxemia.
items.explanation: Patients do not usually develop the acute respiratory failure and significant hypoxemia seen in acute eosinophilic pneumonia.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · CHRONIC EOSINOPHILIC PNEUMONIA

items.statement: Corticosteroids are effective in reducing peripheral and BAL eosinophilia in chronic eosinophilic pneumonia.
items.explanation: Both peripheral and BAL eosinophilia are very responsive to treatment with corticosteroids.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · CHRONIC EOSINOPHILIC PNEUMONIA

items.statement: Cardiac involvement in EGPA often portends a worse prognosis.
items.explanation: Cardiac involvement is a primary target and often indicates a worse prognosis.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · EOSINOPHILIC GRANULOMATOSIS WITH POLYANGIITIS (EGPA) (part 1)

items.statement: Renal involvement in EGPA occurs in less than 10% of patients.
items.explanation: Renal involvement is more common, affecting approximately 25% of patients.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · EOSINOPHILIC GRANULOMATOSIS WITH POLYANGIITIS (EGPA) (part 1)

items.statement: Corticosteroids are associated with a reduced incidence of relapse in EGPA.
items.explanation: Corticosteroids dramatically alter the course of EGPA, improving survival and reducing mortality.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · EOSINOPHILIC GRANULOMATOSIS WITH POLYANGIITIS (EGPA) (part 2)

items.statement: Untreated patients with EGPA have a high survival rate within the first three months.
items.explanation: Up to 50% of untreated EGPA patients die within 3 months of diagnosis.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · EOSINOPHILIC GRANULOMATOSIS WITH POLYANGIITIS (EGPA) (part 2)

items.statement: The myeloproliferative variant of hypereosinophilic syndromes is often associated with a JAK2 mutation.
items.explanation: Mutations involving JAK2 are mentioned as being associated with the myeloproliferative variant of HES.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · HYPEREOSINOPHILIC SYNDROMES

items.statement: Immunoglobulin A (IgA) is typically elevated in lymphocytic hypereosinophilic syndromes.
items.explanation: Elevated IgE, not IgA, may be seen in lymphocytic HES variants.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · HYPEREOSINOPHILIC SYNDROMES

items.statement: ABPA can be a complication of cystic fibrosis.
items.explanation: ABPA is well described as a complication in patients with cystic fibrosis.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · ALLERGIC BRONCHOPULMONARY ASPERGILLOSIS

items.statement: Central bronchiectasis must be present on chest imaging to diagnose ABPA.
items.explanation: While central bronchiectasis is a classic finding, it is not necessary for diagnosing ABPA.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · ALLERGIC BRONCHOPULMONARY ASPERGILLOSIS

items.subtopic: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophilia | INTRODUCTION AND DEFINITION | OFFENDING ANTIGENS
items.assertion: Farmer's lung is associated with exposure to bacterial or fungal antigens.
items.reason: Farmer's lung occurs due to exposure to grain, moldy hay, or silage containing these antigens.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophilia | INTRODUCTION AND DEFINITION | OFFENDING ANTIGENS

items.assertion: The presence of precipitating IgG antibodies against specific antigens in HP suggests a prominent role for adaptive immunity.
items.reason: Innate immune mechanisms, such as activation of Toll-like receptors and MyD88, also contribute to the pathophysiology of HP.
items.explanation: Both statements are true; however, the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.assertion: Chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP) can be diagnosed easily due to its distinct and acute onset of symptoms.
items.reason: The insidious onset of symptoms in chronic HP makes diagnosing it a challenge.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because chronic HP has an insidious onset, making diagnosis difficult.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.assertion: Bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage is specific to hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
items.reason: BAL lymphocytosis is characteristic of HP but not specific.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; BAL findings are suggestive but not exclusive to HP.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.assertion: High-resolution computed tomography can be normal in nonfibrotic hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
items.reason: This may occur due to lack of temporal correlation between antigen exposure and imaging.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Antigen avoidance is the mainstay of treatment for hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
items.reason: Glucocorticoid therapy can accelerate symptom resolution in severe cases.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but antigen avoidance being the mainstay does not depend on glucocorticoid therapy.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis

items.assertion: Organic toxic dust syndrome requires prior antigen sensitization.
items.reason: OTDS occurs with exposure to organic dusts, including those produced by grains or mold silage.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; OTDS does not require prior antigen sensitization.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis

items.assertion: Acute eosinophilic pneumonia has an excellent prognosis with complete recovery.
items.reason: The condition is highly responsive to corticosteroid therapy, leading to rapid clinical and radiographic improvement.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why acute eosinophilic pneumonia has an excellent prognosis.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | ACUTE EOSINOPHILIC PNEUMONIA

items.assertion: The mean age at diagnosis for EGPA is 48 years.
items.reason: EGPA typically occurs in several phases, beginning with asthma and allergic rhinitis in the twenties or thirties.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the mean age at diagnosis.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · EOSINOPHILIC GRANULOMATOSIS WITH POLYANGIITIS (EGPA) (part 1)

items.assertion: Systemic eosinophilia is a hallmark laboratory finding in EGPA.
items.reason: Eosinophilia >1000 cells/μL or >10% of peripheral WBC count reflects the pathogenic role of eosinophils.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains why systemic eosinophilia is a hallmark finding.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · EOSINOPHILIC GRANULOMATOSIS WITH POLYANGIITIS (EGPA) (part 1)

items.assertion: ABPA requires elevated circulating levels of IgE for diagnosis.
items.reason: Elevated IgE is a characteristic finding in ABPA, distinguishing it from simple asthma.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the elevated IgE level helps distinguish ABPA.
items.reference: Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis and Pulmonary Infiltrates with Eosinophil · ALLERGIC BRONCHOPULMONARY ASPERGILLOSIS
